focus_areas: Zone map skipping: Lineitem sorted by l_shipdate with zone maps. Date filter [1994-01-01, 1995-01-01) spans exactly 1 year. Likely spans 126 zone map blocks (1 per ~3 days). Skip blocks outside range to reduce I/O by ~98%.

Compound filter order: Apply filters in selectivity order: (1) l_shipdate >= / < (33% selectivity), (2) l_discount BETWEEN (9% selectivity), (3) l_quantity < 24 (48% selectivity). Combined: 0.33 * 0.09 * 0.48 = 1.4% match rate. Order filters to fail fast on most rows.

SIMD vectorized filtering: Vectorize compound predicates using AVX-512. Process 8 rows at a time with 3 comparisons (date, discount, quantity) per row using vector masks. Branch-free predicate logic (combine conditions with AND, use mask to filter).

Single aggregation (SUM): After filtering, single SUM operation. Use SIMD horizontal reduction (add 8 values per instruction) to accumulate final result. Parallelism: each thread sums its filtered rows into local accumulator, final merge is trivial (one SUM per thread).

Parallelism: Morsel-driven execution on 64 cores. Each thread processes 10K-100K row batch, filters in parallel, accumulates local sum. Global merge: simple addition of 64 partial sums (negligible overhead).

Memory access: Since zone maps eliminate 98% of blocks, filtered result fits in L3 cache. Memory bandwidth no longer bottleneck—CPU compute becomes limiting factor. Ensure SIMD utilization is maximized.
Compile tight loop: Template specialization for Q6-specific code: filter 8 rows at a time, accumulate in AVX-512 vector, horizontal add for each morsel.
strategy_notes: Q6 is highly selective (1.9% match) on a single table. Strategy: (1) Zone map skipping is THE key optimization—skip 98% of blocks, reducing I/O from 60M rows to ~1.1M rows. (2) Vectorize compound filters: date + discount + quantity as single SIMD operation (8 rows per instruction, 3 predicates per row = 24 conditions per instruction). (3) Parallelization: morsel-driven execution with 64 cores, each summing its filtered rows into local variable, then merge 64 partial sums (negligible cost). (4) Avoid branches in filter loop—use mask-based filtering and conditional accumulation. (5) Since result is tiny after filtering, no intermediate materialization needed. Target: 15-20ms (vs DuckDB's 19-21ms baseline), beating DuckDB requires ~20% improvement in filter+aggregation efficiency via SIMD/parallelism.
reasoning: TPC-H SF10 is I/O and CPU bound on 60M lineitem scans. DuckDB achieves 86-113ms for Q1, 78-113ms for Q3, 19-21ms for Q6. Key opportunities: (1) Morsel-driven parallel execution on 64 cores for lineitem scans (7-8x speedup potential), (2) SIMD vectorized filtering and aggregation for hot loops, (3) Zone maps to skip blocks in Q6/Q1, (4) Selective join ordering in Q3 (filter customer→orders→lineitem) to minimize intermediates.
